title: User provisioning sources
description: The five recognised entry points into `UserProvisioner::provision()`. Each source pins a different set of preconditions + notification behaviour — the observer + listeners fan out per source when deciding whether to send email verification, whether to require admin approval, etc.
column_note: Not persisted directly on the User row — stored as `metadata.provision_source` when the caller supplies it. Analytics events include the source as a top-level property.

sources.description: The most common path. An `access::Invitation` row was accepted; the acceptance flow invokes `ProvisionUser` synchronously (in-request) or via `ProvisionUserJob` (async retry).
sources.requires_email_verification: inherited from invitation (usually true; false when the invitation was for a pre-verified partner-federated identity)
sources.initial_status: active (email already verified via the invitation click)

sources.description: Anonymous caller posted to `POST /api/v1/users` with the invitation-free sign-up URL. Requires `user.registration.self_registration_enabled = true` AND `user.registration.requires_invitation = false` on the tenant.
An admin invoked `POST /api/v1/users` explicitly. Admin can pre-verify the email (skips the verification flow) via a `pre_verified: true` request flag.
sources.requires_email_verification: false when admin sets pre_verified=true; otherwise true
sources.initial_status: active OR pending (depends on admin approval setting)

sources.description: Identity provisioned this User in response to a cross-app SSO grant exchange (design.md §7.4). The target Application receives the grant + provisions a fresh User backed by the same underlying Identity.
CSV bulk-invite via `POST /api/v1/users/bulk`. Each CSV row creates an `access::Invitation`; accepted invitations invoke `ProvisionUser` via `ProvisionUserJob` async.
sources.quota: 1 unit of `bulk_user_provision` per row (regardless of whether the invitation is accepted)
